fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build that database read-routing thing we've been talking about for ages. The basic idea is: we have primary and replica DB nodes, and right now everything hits the primary which is getting hammered. We want a way to wrap chunks of code so reads inside that wrapper go to the replica instead. Pretty similar to how we handled the connection-scoping stuff in the old session middleware, if you remember that pattern.

There should also be a global 'just distribute everything by default' toggle for apps that want to go all-in. Key thing though — after someone does a write, we can't serve their next read from a stale replica, that's caused support tickets before.

We also need some kind of staleness protection — if the replica is too far behind, we should either blow up loudly or quietly fall back to primary depending on config. Same deal if there are no replicas at all. Background jobs should also play nicely with this.

One thing I keep forgetting to mention: there's a lazy query issue where people return unexecuted query objects from the wrapper and then wonder why the replica isn't being used — we should warn about that.

Edge cases like calling the wrapper without actually passing it a block of work, or passing in option keys we don't recognize, should produce clear errors. Let me know if anything's unclear, I'll be around.

One quick follow-up with the extra specifics people asked for. If the distribution entry point gets called without a block, it should raise the missing-block case and the output needs to be exactly two lines: `error=missing_block` and then `message=Missing block`. Same idea for bad option keys: if someone opens the distribution scope with keys we don’t recognize, raise the unknown-keywords case and return `error=unknown_keywords` followed by `message=Unknown keywords: <key1>, <key2>`, with the offending keys listed in the same order they were passed in.

On routing behavior, a scope opened with `replica: true` should send reads in that scope to the replica even if default-distribution mode is off, and reads outside that scope should still use the primary. Also, SQL comment prefixes, meaning the `prefix` field on a read op, should not influence routing at all. On the flip side, when default-distribution mode is on, a scope opened with `primary: true` should override that default and keep all reads in that scope on the primary.

For lag reporting, the `replication_lag` operation should return the current replica lag as a numeric value and render it as `replication_lag=<value>`, for example `replication_lag=2`.

One subtle but important bit on the stale-read protection: in default-distribution mode (`by_default: true`), if a write happens outside any explicit scope, then every later top-level read needs to stay pinned to the primary, not the replica. But inside an explicit distribution scope, a write does not pin later reads in that same scope; those reads still go to the replica. Then when that scope ends, any write that happened before the scope was entered starts mattering again for top-level reads.

Also, any read inside a transaction block must always go to the primary, even if that transaction is nested inside a distribution scope. Transactional work needs to stay on the writable node. And to make all of this work consistently, we do need the distribution scope/context-state piece that keeps track of whether a scope is active, whether a write has happened, and whether a transaction is currently open, basically the same kind of thread-local or stack-style context tracking we’ve used before in the core domain.

trigger_keywords: replica lag threshold
max lag exceeded
lag over limit error
replication lag too high
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a `max_lag` threshold is set and the measured lag exceeds it, the scope must raise before any read runs. Output: `error=too_much_lag` then `message=Replica lag over <N> seconds`. If the lag cannot be determined (null/nil), the message is instead `Replica lag is nil on <connection_name>` where the default connection renders as `default`.

trigger_keywords: lag failover behavior
fall back on high lag
lag_failover option
log instead of raise on lag
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When `lag_failover: true` is set and the lag exceeds the threshold (or is unknown), the scope must NOT raise. Instead it logs a message as `log=Replica lag over <N> seconds. Falling back to master pool.` (or `log=Replica lag is nil on <connection>. Falling back to master pool.` for unknown lag) and then routes all reads in that scope to the primary.

trigger_keywords: named connection lag check
lag_on option
check lag for specific connection
lag on User connection
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The lag check can be targeted at a named connection via `lag_on: "<Name>"` (or a list). When that connection's lag exceeds the threshold, the error message must name the connection: `Replica lag over <N> seconds on <Name> connection`.

trigger_keywords: no replicas available
all replicas blacklisted
failover to primary when no replicas
replica unavailable behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no replicas are available (blacklist_replicas scenario), the default behavior is to log `log=No replicas available. Falling back to master pool.` and route reads to the primary. If `failover: false` is explicitly set in the scope options, instead raise: `error=no_replicas_available` with `message=No replicas available`.

trigger_keywords: background job routing
job context reset
job distribution mode
read_only job replica
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Background jobs declared with a `read_only` role route their reads to the replica, rendering as `job=replica`. In default-distribution mode, a job with a `read_write` role also runs its read on the replica on each invocation because job context is reset between runs — a write in one job run does not pin reads in the next run.

trigger_keywords: lazy query warning
unexecuted query object returned from scope
to_a warning
relation not executed inside block
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a distribution scope body contains a `lazy_read` operation (a query object that has not been executed yet is returned), the library must log a warning: `log=Call \`to_a\` inside block to execute query on replica`. This advises the caller to force execution inside the scope.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: after someone does a write, we can't serve their next read from a stale replica, that's caused support tickets before
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The write-pinning behavior in default-distribution mode (by_default: true): a write at the top level sets a sticky flag that routes subsequent reads to the primary; this flag is scoped so it does not bleed into explicit distribution scopes (feature3_1 and feature3_2 test cases).

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have the specific detail on that right now — could you check the existing test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` or the PRD in `start.md`? Those should have the exact behavior defined. If something still seems ambiguous after checking, flag it and we can discuss.

feature10_1_missing_block.json.description: Invoking the distribution entry point without a block raises a missing-block error.

feature10_2_unknown_keywords.json.description: Invoking the distribution entry point with options it does not recognize raises an unknown-keywords error listing the offending keys in order.

feature1_default_routing.json.description: With no distribution scope active and default mode off, a read is served by the primary database.

feature2_1_block_reads_replica.json.description: A read executed outside any scope uses the primary; inside a distribution scope reads use the replica, and a write performed inside the scope does not pin later reads back to the primary.

feature2_2_transaction_primary.json.description: A read wrapped in a transaction inside a distribution scope is served by the primary, because transactional work must stay on the writable node.

feature3_1_by_default_sticky.json.description: When default-distribution mode is on, a plain read uses the replica; after a write, subsequent reads are pinned to the primary for consistency.

feature3_2_block_inside_default.json.description: With default-distribution mode on, reads inside a distribution scope stay on the replica even after a write within the scope; once the scope exits, an earlier write pins the next read to the primary.

feature3_3_primary_override.json.description: With default-distribution mode on, opening a scope that forces the primary routes its reads back to the primary.

feature4_1_max_lag_exceeded.json.description: When a maximum replica-lag threshold is set and the measured lag exceeds it, opening the scope raises a lag-exceeded error before any read runs. The threshold may be supplied per scope or via global defaults.

feature4_2_max_lag_under.json.description: When a maximum replica-lag threshold is set and the measured lag is at or below it (whether stubbed low or measured live), reads proceed on the replica.

feature4_3_lag_unknown.json.description: When the replica lag cannot be determined (unknown/nil) and a lag threshold is set, opening the scope raises a lag-exceeded error naming the connection.

cases.expected_output:
error=too_much_lag
message=Replica lag is nil on default

feature4_4_lag_failover.json.description: When lag failover is enabled, exceeding the lag threshold (or being unable to measure lag) does not raise; instead a message is logged and reads fall back to the primary.

cases.expected_output:
log=Replica lag over 1 seconds. Falling back to master pool.
read=primary

log=Replica lag is nil on default. Falling back to master pool.
read=primary

feature4_5_lag_on_connection.json.description: When the lag threshold is checked against a named connection (supplied singly or as a list) and that connection's lag exceeds the threshold, the raised error names that connection.

cases.expected_output:
error=too_much_lag
message=Replica lag over 1 seconds on User connection

feature5_replication_lag.json.description: The current replica replication lag can be read as a numeric value.

feature6_1_failover_to_primary.json.description: When no replicas are available, a distribution scope logs the situation and falls back to the primary. This holds whether or not default-distribution mode is on.

cases.expected_output:
log=No replicas available. Falling back to master pool.
read=primary

feature6_2_failover_disabled.json.description: When no replicas are available and failover is disabled, opening the scope raises a no-replicas-available error. This holds whether or not default-distribution mode is on.

feature7_1_force_replica.json.description: Forcing replica use for a scope routes its reads to the replica even when default-distribution mode is off; reads outside the scope still use the primary. SQL comment prefixes do not change routing.

feature7_2_force_replica_failover.json.description: Forcing replica use when no replicas are available logs the situation and falls back to the primary.

cases.expected_output:
log=No replicas available. Falling back to master pool.
read=primary

feature7_3_force_replica_failover_disabl.description: Forcing replica use when no replicas are available and failover is disabled raises a no-replicas-available error.

feature8_1_job_distribution.json.description: A background job declared to distribute its reads runs its reads on the replica.

feature8_2_job_by_default.json.description: With default-distribution mode on, a background job that both reads and writes runs its read on the replica on each invocation, because job context is reset between runs.

feature9_lazy_relation_warning.json.description: When a distribution scope returns a query object that has not been executed yet, a warning is logged advising the caller to force execution inside the scope.

cases.expected_output: log=Call `to_a` inside block to execute query on replica
